definition: Preeclampsia is a serious blood pressure condition that develops during pregnancy, typically after 20 weeks. It is characterized by high blood pressure and often signs of damage to another organ system, most often the liver and kidneys. If left untreated, preeclampsia can lead to serious—even fatal—complications for both the mother and the baby.

Body mass index (BMI) is a medical screening tool that measures the ratio of a person's weight to their height to estimate body fat. It is calculated by dividing weight in kilograms by the square of height in meters. BMI is used to broadly categorize individuals as underweight, healthy weight, overweight, or obese, which can help assess risks for health conditions like heart disease and type 2 diabetes.

The variable uta_pi_l refers to the left uterine artery pulsatility index, a measure used in Doppler ultrasound to assess blood flow resistance in the left uterine artery. In the context of pregnancy, an elevated pulsatility index can indicate impaired placental perfusion and is often used as a marker to predict the risk of developing preeclampsia or fetal growth restriction.
The presence of bilateral notches in the uterine artery Doppler waveform.
Gestational age refers to the length of time a pregnancy has lasted, typically measured in weeks from the first day of the person's last menstrual period.
Birth weight refers to the first weight of a newborn obtained after birth. In clinical studies, it is often used as a primary outcome measure to assess fetal growth and neonatal health.

Soluble fms-like tyrosine kinase 1 (sFlt-1) is an antiangiogenic protein produced by the placenta that inhibits vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and placental growth factor (PlGF). In cases of preeclampsia, sFlt-1 levels are significantly increased, leading to maternal endothelial dysfunction. The ratio of sFlt-1 to PlGF is used as a clinical biomarker to aid in the risk assessment and short-term prediction of preeclampsia with severe features.

Placental growth factor (PlGF) is a protein produced by the placenta that promotes the development of new blood vessels. In clinical practice, measuring maternal blood levels of PlGF helps predict and diagnose preeclampsia, as low levels indicate placental dysfunction and an increased risk of the condition.

The sFlt-1/PlGF ratio is the ratio of soluble fms-like tyrosine kinase 1 to placental growth factor. It is a clinical biomarker used to predict the risk of developing preeclampsia and related adverse outcomes by identifying an imbalance between antiangiogenic and proangiogenic factors in maternal serum.
